content: Men were walking about the plain and talking.
Everybody seemed much excited.
"Eric killed the men in fair fight.
The judges outlawed him because they were afraid.
His foe Thorgest has many rich and powerful men to back him."
"Eric is a bloody man.
"He walks like Thor the Fearless."
"His story would make a fine song," one said.
Always in a quarrel.
A man of many places-Norway, the north of Iceland, the west of Iceland, those little islands off the shore of Iceland.
"He is in a black temper," they said.
"We should best not talk to him."
Eric got into the pilot's seat and they sailed off.
Soon they pulled the ship up on their own shore. Eric strolled into his house and called for supper.
When the drinking horns had been filled and emptied, Eric pulled himself up and smiled and shouted out so that the great room was full of his big voice:
"There is no friend like mead.
Then laughter and talking began in the hall because Eric's good temper had come back.
After a while Eric said:
"Well, I must off somewhere.
I have been driven about from place to place, like a seabird in a storm.
She has shut Norway to me and now Iceland. Where will you go next, old comrade?" and he pulled out his sword and looked at it and smiled as the fire flashed on it.
"Is it so?" Eric cried, leaping up.
Who will go with me?"
Eric sat down in his chair and laughed.
He jumped to his feet, and his face glowed.
Then he laughed as he looked at his men.
His men pounded on the tables and shouted:
"Yes!
Yes!
Anywhere behind Eric!"
Huge ships of ice sailed out from it and met him.
A great shout went up that shook the rafters.
Then the men sat and talked over plans.
While they sat, a stranger came into the hall.
"Hunted like a wolf from corner to corner of the world!" Eric cried angrily.
Then he laughed.
"But if I take my sport like a wolf, I must be hunted like one.
Well, we have done it before."
"I always liked the stars better than a smoky house fire," said one.
"Can no bad fortune spoil your good nature?" laughed Eric.
Let every man carry what he can."
Eric led his wife Thorhild and his two young sons, Thorstein and Leif.
"Thorgest is off.
Come to my house for a feast."
All this time they were making ready for the voyage, repairing the ship and filling it with stores.
Word of what Eric meant to do got out, and men laughed and said:
"Is that not like Eric?
What will he not do?"
Some men liked the sound of it, and they came to Eric and said:
"We will go with you to this strange land."
They took horses and cattle with them, and all kinds of tools and food.
So I will steer straight west.
We shall surely find something.
I do not know, either, how long we must go."
One day as Eric sat in the pilot's seat, he said:
"Yes," shouted his men.
So they went on toward it.
"It sends out a cold breath," said one of the men.
They all wrapped their cloaks about them.
"The white mast stands as high as a hill."
"It must be giants that sail in it, frost giants," said another of the men.
But as they came nearer, Eric all at once laughed loudly and called out:
"By Thor, that Gunnbiorn was a foolish fellow.
Why, look!
It is no ship, and there is no one on it."
"And you thought of frost giants!"
"Yonder is a strange pilot," Eric laughed.
One day Eric said:
"I see afar off an iceberg larger than any one yet.
He bore hard on the rudder, but he could not turn the ship.
It is carrying our ship away from this land.
I cannot make head against it. Out with the oars!"
"Perhaps this river will carry us to a whirlpool and suck us under," the men said.
When the men looked above the cliffs they saw a long line of white cutting the sky.
"It is a land of ice," they said.
They sailed on south, all the time looking for a place to go ashore.
All the time the weather was growing colder. Eric's people kept themselves wrapped in their cloaks and put scarfs around their heads.
"And it is still summer!" Thorhild said.
"What will it be in winter?"
"We must not freeze here."
They ate the meat and wore the skins to keep them warm. The hardest thing was to get fuel for the fire.
No trees grew there.
I am tired of the darkness and the smoke and the cattle.
"Ah, cheer up, good wife!" Eric laughed.
"I smell better luck ahead."
When they came home they had this to tell:
"It is a country of ice, shining white.
Far off it looks flat, but when you walk upon it, there are great holes and cracks.
Sometimes the sun showed for an hour, sometimes for only a few minutes, sometimes it did not show at all for a week.
At last they found a place that Eric liked.
"Here I will build my house," he said.
So they did and lived there that summer and pastured their cattle and cut hay for the winter and fished and hunted.
The next spring Eric said:
"The land stretches far north.
"We cannot tell what might come between us.
Perhaps giants or dragons or strange men might come out of this inland ice and kill our people.
We must stay together."
One spring after they had been in that land for four years, Eric said:
I will visit my friends in Iceland."
"Perhaps we can find play for them in Iceland."
"Now I have a plan," Eric suddenly said.
Now we must have a name for our land.
His men laughed and said:
"It is a very white Greenland, but men will like the sound of it.
It is better than Iceland."
"Ah! Eric, it is good to hear your laugh again," they said.
Come back with me and choose your land."
Some men went because they thought it would be a great frolic to go to a new country.
Some went because they were poor in Iceland and thought:
And some went because they loved Eric and wanted to be his neighbors.
But they met heavy storms, and some ships were wrecked, and the men drowned.
So of those thirty five ships only fifteen got to Greenland.
"Only the bravest and the luckiest men come here," Eric said.
Soon other houses were built along the fiords.
"It is pleasant to sail along the coast now," said Eric.
One spring Leif said to his father:
"I have never seen Norway, our mother land.
"It is right that you should go.
So he helped Leif fit out a boat and sent him off.
Leif sailed for months.
He passed Iceland and the Faroes and the Shetlands.
He stopped at all of these places and feasted his mind on the new things.
"I will go to the king," he said.
He had never seen a king.
There were no kings in Iceland or in Greenland.
Now the king was going to hold a feast at night, and Leif put on his most beautiful clothes to go to it.
He belted his jacket with a gold girdle.
He had shoes of scarlet with golden clasps.
On his head he put a knitted cap of bright colors.
"He is surely worth knowing.
His face is not the face of a fool.
He carries his head like a lord of men."
"I am glad to have you for a guest," the king said.
"From Greenland!" said the king.
Come up and speak with me."
You are a welcome guest."
When he started back in the spring, the king gave him two thralls as a parting gift.
"Let this gift show my love, Leif Ericsson," he said.
"For your sake I shall not forget Greenland."
They did not know where they were.
"Surely luck has brought us also to a new country.
So he steered for it.
As they came near, the men said:
Surely this is a better country than Greenland or than Iceland either."
"There is no stone here as in Norway, but only good black dirt," Leif said.
The men were hungry and set about building a fire.
"There is no lack of fuel here," they said.
A German, named Tyrker, was with Leif.
Now one day they had been wandering about and all came back to camp at night except Tyrker.
Then Leif was angry.
"Why did you not keep together?
Why did you not set out to look for him?
Then he turned and started out to hunt for him.
His men followed, silent and ashamed.
He was laughing and talking to himself.
"Why are you so late?" he asked.
"I have not been so very far, but I have found something wonderful."
"What is it?" cried the men.
"I have found grapes growing wild," answered Tyrker, and he laughed, and his eyes shone.
Grapes do not grow in Greenland nor in Iceland nor even in Norway.
"Can I not tell grapes when I see them?" cried Tyrker.
"It is wonderful," Leif said.
You shall take us to them early in the morning, Tyrker."
"They are like food and drink," they cried.
We must start back.
The ship we will load with logs from these great trees.
Now half of you shall gather grapes for the next few days, and the other half shall cut timber."
So they did, and after a week sailed off.
As they looked back at the shore, Leif said:
"I will call this country Wineland for the grapes that grow there."
The Greenlanders looked.
"Lumber! lumber!" they cried.
Then they saw the grapes and tasted them.
"Surely you must have plundered Asgard," they said, smacking their lips.
At the feast that night Eric said:
"Leif shall sit in the place of honor."
"No man would ever need a cloak there.
The forests are without end.
The sea is full of fish."
They turned and talked to Leif's ship comrades who were scattered among them.
Leif noticed two strangers, an old man who sat at Eric's side and a young woman on the cross bench.
He turned to his brother Thorstein who sat next to him.
"Who are these strangers?" he asked.
"They landed here this spring.
But ill luck came, and he grew poor.
This hurt his pride.
'I will not stay in Iceland and be a beggar,' he said to himself.
"Then he got ready a great feast and invited all his friends.
For he said to himself, 'I will not leave in shame.
I mean to marry her, if her father will permit it."
But people could not forget his story.
